text: The video demonstrates the construction of an ileorectal anastomosis using a single-port laparoscopic technique, showcasing the surgical approach and instrument manipulation within the abdominal cavity.
The patient has previously had a total colectomy done as an emergency for Crohn's disease and has an end ileostomy with the rectal stump.
The surgeon fully mobilizes the end ileostomy and places the anvil of a circular stapler into its lumen.
The surgeon returns the distal ileum into the peritoneal cavity and places an Alexis wound protector at the site of the stoma.
A glove is placed over the outer ring of the wound protector, with laparoscopic trocars inserted into the glove fingers, converting the stoma site into a single-port portal.

The laparoscopic view through the surgical glove port at the mobilized end ileostomy site reveals the top of the distal colorectal stump, which was previously buried subfascially during the patient's prior total colectomy for acute severe colitis.

The fact that the distal sigmoid along with its mesocolon are also still in situ makes this procedure quite straightforward in many regards, as does the fact that the index procedure was performed laparoscopically, minimizing the extent of interperitoneal adhesions present.
The surgeon begins the intra-abdominal procedure by mobilizing the top of the colorectal stump, which was previously buried subfascially.
The surgeon uses a thirty-degree video scope to manipulate and achieve excellent visualization of the surgical field, enhancing the view of the intra-abdominal structures.
The surgeon uses a ligature device along with a retracting instrument to apply downward traction on the colorectal stump, efficiently mobilizing it within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs cross-stapling on the colorectal stump, creating a fresh and well-vascularized end in preparation for the iliorectal anastomosis.
If the rectal stump has been shortened and left intraperitoneally at the first operation, this step may not be necessary.
The surgeon uses a sixty millimeter laparoscopic stapler to fully transect the colorectal stump, completing the cross-stapling process.
The surgeon places the stapler directly into one of the glove port fingers, which is then tied off after removing the stapler to avoid using an additional 12mm laparoscopic trocar.
The surgeon withdraws the fully transected cuff of the colorectum into the little finger of the surgical glove port, which remains unused during the procedure.
The surgeon clamps off the glove port finger containing the specimen, maintaining pneumoperitoneum by avoiding glove removal from the wound protector.
The surgeon assesses the length of the ileum, ensuring that the distal ileum with the anvil in place can reach the top of the distal stump for a tension-free anastomosis.
The surgeon performs further dissection to separate interloop adhesions that are restricting the mobility of the small bowel, aiming to facilitate a tension-free anastomosis.
The surgeon carefully examines the small bowel, fully dividing all adhesions between the bowel and its mesentery to enhance mobility.
The surgeon completes the dissection of interloop adhesions, restoring mobility to the small bowel.
The head of the circular stapler is inserted per anode, with its spike emerging just posterior to the cross staple line.
Under direct endoscopic vision, the surgeon mates the head of the circular stapler with the spike of the anvil stapler to construct the anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a sigmoidoscope to descend the submerged anastomosis, testing its integrity by observing for any leakage of air bubbles.
The surgeon closes the stoma site incision using sutures, completing the surgical procedure.
